understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by arranging or moving objects so that at least one pushable object ended up on a goal tile marked with '+', or otherwise satisfying a level-specific condition reported by the engine as WIN. Once any level produced a WIN result, the entire game run would be considered successfully completed.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: Movement was controlled through directional actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) applied to a currently selected piece. The selected piece could be changed by using CLICK on a coordinate, which immediately teleported the selection cursor (and possibly the viewpoint) to that piece’s position; the piece itself did not move between squares by CLICK, but our focus changed to another existing object at that location. Movement would only succeed if the target tile was free of blocking obstacles or walls; otherwise the engine reported that the movement had no effect. Pushing occurred when the selected movable piece attempted to move into a tile occupied by a pushable object and the space beyond was free, causing the pushable object (and possibly chains of such objects) to be pushed along. If the tile in front of a pushable object was blocked (by a wall, the edge of the grid, or a non-pushable object), pushing could not occur and the move was blocked. Walls and other solid obstacles acted as impassable tiles that prevented both movement and pushing. Selection did not consume movement in the spatial sense but still cost steps in the run’s step budget, and every MOVE_* or CLICK reduced the remaining steps. The run ended when this step budget reached zero, regardless of whether the win condition had been met.

The world is a 15x15 cell grid wrapped in a larger wall border. Each selectable is 3x3 pixels (1 cell). Movement is blocked if the adjacent cells intersect walls. history.summary: Level 1 step budget exhausted.
Agent explained goal and mechanics.

understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by arranging or moving objects so that at least one pushable object ended up on a goal tile marked with '+', or otherwise satisfying a level-specific condition reported by the engine as WIN. Once any level produced a WIN result, the entire game run would be considered successfully completed.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: Movement was controlled through directional actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) applied to a currently selected piece. The selected piece could be changed by using CLICK on a coordinate, which immediately teleported the selection cursor (and possibly the viewpoint) to that piece’s position; the piece itself did not move between squares by CLICK, but our focus changed to another existing object at that location. Movement would only succeed if the target tile was free of blocking obstacles or walls; otherwise the engine reported that the movement had no effect. Pushing occurred when the selected movable piece attempted to move into a tile occupied by a pushable object and the space beyond was free, causing the pushable object (and possibly chains of such objects) to be pushed along. If the tile in front of a pushable object was blocked (by a wall, the edge of the grid, or a non-pushable object), pushing could not occur and the move was blocked. Walls and other solid obstacles acted as impassable tiles that prevented both movement and pushing. Selection did not consume movement in the spatial sense but still cost steps in the run’s step budget, and every MOVE_* or CLICK reduced the remaining steps. The run ended when this step budget reached zero, regardless of whether the win condition had been met.
